label: Seborrheic dermatitis or dandruff without secondary infection
summary: Your symptoms fit seborrheic dermatitis or dandruff.
found: Your exam was reassuring for this common flaky skin condition. We did not find signs of a deeper infection or emergency skin problem today.
home: Use the shampoo, cream, or skin-care plan your clinician recommended.
Avoid scratching or picking at irritated areas.
Keep the area clean and avoid harsh products that make burning or itching worse.
meds: Use over-the-counter dandruff shampoo or medicated shampoo only as directed on the label or by your clinician.
Use acetaminophen or ibuprofen only if you can take it safely and follow the label.
return: Spreading redness, warmth, pus, fever, or severe pain.
Rash near the eye, rapidly worsening swelling, or skin peeling.
Symptoms become much worse despite the plan you were given.
follow: Follow up with primary care or dermatology if symptoms are not improving with the plan you were given.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of seborrheic dermatitis or dandruff.
No secondary bacterial infection, eye involvement, severe diffuse rash, or immunocompromised-host pathway.
exclusion: Rash near the eye or mucosal involvement.
Severe diffuse rash, skin peeling, systemic illness, or immunocompromised host requiring a separate pathway.
must_not_miss: Psoriasis flare requiring a different pathway.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports seborrheic dermatitis and dandruff framing, common symptoms, medicated-shampoo home care, chronic relapsing course, and escalation for failed self-care or drainage, pus, crusting, marked redness, or pain. Autonomous Phase 1501-1550 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed seborrheic dermatitis or dandruff without secondary infection, eye involvement, mucosal disease, severe diffuse rash, systemic illness, or immunocompromised-host pathway.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of seborrheic dermatitis or dandruff
no eye, mucosal, severe diffuse, systemic, or immunocompromised-host pathway

label: Uncomplicated body ringworm without cellulitis
summary: Your symptoms fit ringworm of the skin, also called tinea corporis.
found: Your exam was reassuring for a localized fungal skin infection. We did not find signs of cellulitis, an abscess, or a deeper emergency infection today.
home: Keep the area clean and dry.
Avoid sharing towels, clothing, or sports gear until the rash is improving.
Wash your hands after touching the rash.
meds: Use the antifungal cream, spray, or powder exactly as directed on the label or by your clinician.
Do not use steroid cream on the rash unless your clinician specifically told you to.
return: Spreading redness, warmth, pus, fever, or severe pain.
The rash spreads quickly, involves the scalp or nails, or keeps coming back.
You feel very ill or your immune system is weakened.
follow: Follow up with primary care or dermatology if the rash is not improving with treatment or if it returns.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized tinea corporis or body ringworm.
No cellulitis, abscess, scalp/nail/beard involvement, extensive disease, or immunocompromised-host pathway.
exclusion: Cellulitis, abscess, or secondary infection concern.
Scalp, nail, beard, groin, or foot fungal pathway requiring different instructions.
Extensive, recurrent, treatment-failure, or immunocompromised-host disease.
source_audit_notes: CDC supports ringworm and tinea framing, contagious spread precautions, antifungal treatment concepts, and special handling for scalp, nail, extensive, persistent, recurrent, or high-risk disease. Autonomous Phase 1501-1550 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed localized body ringworm without cellulitis, abscess, scalp/nail/beard/groin/foot pathway, systemic illness, treatment failure, or immunocompromised-host pathway.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized tinea corporis or body ringworm
localized skin-only disease and no cellulitis or abscess documented
no scalp, nail, beard, groin, foot, recurrent, extensive, or immunocompromised-host pathway

label: Ingrown toenail without abscess or cellulitis
summary: Your symptoms fit an ingrown toenail.
found: Your exam was reassuring enough for home care today. We did not find an abscess, spreading cellulitis, or another emergency foot problem.
home: Soak the foot in warm salty water if your clinician said this is safe for you.
Keep the foot dry the rest of the day.
Wear wide, comfortable shoes or sandals.
meds: Use acetaminophen or ibuprofen only if you can take it safely and follow the label.
Take antibiotics only if they were prescribed.
return: Pus, spreading redness, fever, chills, or worsening swelling.
Severe pain, numbness, black or blue skin, or you cannot walk normally.
You have diabetes, poor circulation, or a weakened immune system and symptoms worsen.
follow: Follow up with primary care or podiatry if home care is not helping or if this keeps coming back.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of ingrown toenail.
No abscess, cellulitis, diabetic foot concern, poor circulation concern, nail procedure aftercare pathway, or systemic illness.
exclusion: Abscess, cellulitis, pus, fever, or systemic illness.
Diabetes, poor circulation, immunocompromised host, neuropathy, or diabetic foot pathway.
Recent nail procedure or specialist-directed podiatry plan.
source_audit_notes: NHS supports ingrown-toenail framing, pain/redness/swelling symptoms, warm salty-water soaks, keeping the foot dry, wide footwear, label-safe pain control, and escalation for pus, feverish symptoms, failed home care, diabetes, antibiotics, or podiatry treatment. Autonomous Phase 1501-1550 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed ingrown toenail without abscess, cellulitis, pus, diabetic foot risk, poor circulation, immunocompromised-host pathway, or specialist procedure aftercare.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of ingrown toenail
no abscess, cellulitis, pus, or spreading infection documented
no diabetes, poor circulation, neuropathy, immunocompromised-host, systemic illness, or procedure aftercare pathway

label: Simple canker sore without systemic symptoms
summary: Your symptoms fit a canker sore, also called an aphthous ulcer.
found: Your exam was reassuring for a simple mouth sore. We did not find signs of a dangerous mouth or throat infection today.
home: Avoid spicy, acidic, or sharp foods if they make the sore hurt more.
Brush gently and keep up with routine mouth care.
Drink fluids so mouth pain does not lead to dehydration.
meds: Use over-the-counter mouth pain medicine only as directed on the label or by your clinician.
Use acetaminophen or ibuprofen only if you can take it safely and follow the label.
return: Fever, rash, eye pain, genital sores, severe swelling, or trouble swallowing.
The sore lasts more than 2 weeks, gets worse, or keeps coming back.
follow: Follow up with primary care, dentistry, or ENT if the sore is not improving, lasts more than 2 weeks, or recurs often.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of simple canker sore or aphthous ulcer.
No fever, rash, ocular symptoms, genital ulcers, immunocompromised-host pathway, deep-space infection, or airway concern.
exclusion: Fever, rash, diarrhea, headache with systemic syndrome, ocular symptoms, genital ulcers, or recurrent severe ulcers.
Deep-space dental/neck infection, airway symptoms, dehydration, or immunocompromised host.
Suspicion for herpes, zoster, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, or malignancy.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports canker-sore and aphthous-ulcer framing, expected self-resolution, gentle oral care and symptom relief concepts, and escalation for ulcers lasting more than two weeks, worsening, frequent recurrence, or associated fever, diarrhea, headache, or rash. Autonomous Phase 1501-1550 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed simple canker sore without systemic features, ocular or genital involvement, dehydration, airway/deep-space concern, immunocompromise, or recurrent severe-ulcer pathway.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of simple canker sore or aphthous ulcer
no systemic or dangerous oral features documented
no ocular, genital, airway, deep-space, dehydration, immunocompromised-host, or recurrent severe-ulcer pathway

label: Localized insect bite or sting without systemic reaction
condition_terms: localized insect bite no systemic reaction
summary: Your symptoms fit a localized insect bite or sting reaction.
found: Your exam was reassuring for a local skin reaction. We did not find signs of anaphylaxis, severe venom reaction, or spreading infection today.
home: Wash the area with soap and water.
Use a cold pack wrapped in cloth for short periods if it helps.
meds: Use itch medicine, allergy medicine, or pain medicine only as directed on the label or by your clinician.
Use an epinephrine injector only if one was prescribed and you were instructed to use it.
return: Trouble breathing, wheezing, throat tightness, face or mouth swelling, fainting, or vomiting.
Spreading redness, pus, fever, red streaks, or worsening pain.
A black widow, brown recluse, scorpion, tick-borne illness, or snakebite concern comes up.
follow: Follow up with primary care or allergy clinic if symptoms persist, worsen, or reactions keep happening.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized insect bite or sting reaction.
No anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, systemic reaction, venomous spider/scorpion/snake pathway, tick-borne illness concern, or cellulitis.
exclusion: Anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, hypotension, syncope, vomiting, or bodywide reaction.
Cellulitis, abscess, necrosis, severe pain, or systemic illness.
Black widow, brown recluse, scorpion, tick-borne illness, snakebite, or specialist-directed allergy plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports insect-bite and sting framing, common local symptoms, home care with washing/cold packs/itch treatment, and emergency escalation for trouble breathing, wheezing, face or mouth swelling, throat tightness, swallowing trouble, weakness, or blue color. Autonomous Phase 1501-1550 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed localized bite or sting reaction without anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, hypotension, systemic reaction, cellulitis, venomous spider/scorpion/snake pathway, tick-borne illness concern, or specialist-directed allergy plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized insect bite or sting reaction
no systemic reaction, anaphylaxis, or airway symptoms documented
no cellulitis, necrotic spider, scorpion, tick-borne illness, snakebite, or specialist-directed allergy pathway
